          │

          │   the same Zen proverb

          │   tmp/.pwn` full ROP exploit → `rm -rf /` → `cafebabe-1337-beef` UUID
          │ - Live system data: `/proc/cpuinfo`, `meminfo`, `uptime`, `loadavg`, `uname`
          │   all pulling real values

          │   `kill 1337` blocked, `&&` chaining, pipes, session-persistent `export`, CWD
          │   tracking in prompt

          │   `rm`

          │ happy with, or features you're thinking of adding?

      You │ now actually i think i am in a place where i _finally_ can open up the base
          │ and actually let people contribute without being annoyed at something :). so
          │ are you up for writing a plugin of what you fancy adding?
          │ 
          │ ````
          │ ## make it yours
          │ 
          │ everything you customize without writing new code lives in these files.
          │ framework code under `src/core/` and the non-`me/` plugins under
          │ `src/plugins/` stays untouched.
          │ 
          │ | file                        | what's in it
          │ |
          │ | --------------------------- |
          │ -------------------------------------------------------- |
          │ | `src/config.ts`             | GitHub username, PostHog key, optional
          │ hostname override |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/welcome.ts` | Landing banner + bio + links (the `welcome`
          │ command)     |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/about.ts`   | `about.txt` content + `/bin/about`
          │ |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/contact.ts` | `contact.txt` content
          │ |
          │ | `src/system.ts`             | Fictional OS / hardware / firmware / kernel
          │ identity     |
          │ | `src/themes/index.ts`       | `DEFAULT_THEME` — which theme to boot with
          │ |
          │ 
          │ Recommended path:
          │ 
          │ 1. Clone or use as template.
          │ 2. Edit `src/config.ts`.
          │ 3. Edit each `src/plugins/me/*.ts` — swap in your bio, links, etc.
          │ 4. Pick a default theme in `src/themes/index.ts` (`DEFAULT_THEME`).
          │ 5. Optional: rebrand `src/system.ts`
          │ 
          │ ---
          │ 
          │ **Built-in commands**
          │ 
          │ - `ls`, `cd`, `pwd`, `cat`, `echo`, `file`, `help`, `clear`, `exit`, `restart`
          │ - `mkdir`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, `rm`
          │ - `grep`, `wc`, `find`
          │ - `whoami`, `id`, `history`
          │ - `uname`, `date`, `who`, `ps`, `kill`
          │ - `theme`, `colortest`, `welcome`, `projects`, `version`, `about`
          │ 
          │ **Shell**
          │ 
          │ - Pipelines (`|`), redirection (`>`, `>>`, `<`), chaining (`&&`, `||`, `;`)
          │ - Variable expansion (`$VAR`, `${VAR}`), assignments, environment
          │ - Pathname expansion (`*`, `?`, `[…]`, `~`)
          │ - Quoting (single literal, double with `$` interpolation)
          │ - History persisted to `~/.bash_history` (↑/↓ to walk)
          │ - Tab completion (executables for the first word, paths elsewhere), `Ctrl+L`
          │   to clear
          │ 
          │ ```bash
          │ ls -la /bin | grep un | wc -l
          │ echo hello > ~/note.txt && cat ~/note.txt
          │ NAME=world; echo "hello, $NAME"
          │ ````
          │ 
          │ **Virtual filesystem**
          │ 
          │ - Unix-style permissions (owner/group/mode), per-command identity
          │ - Guest vs root users (if user finds how to escalate)
          │ - Mounts: `/proc` and `/dev` are synthetic; `/etc`, `/home`, `/root`, `/usr`,
          │   `/var` are tree-mounted by dedicated plugins
          │ - `vfs.appendDir(path, children)` lets any plugin contribute files to a tree
          │   owned by another plugin (strict: path must exist; duplicate names throw)
          │ - `resolve`, `list`, `stat`, `read`, `write`, `mkdir`, `rm`: same contract
          │   every plugin uses
          │ 
          │ ## Extending
          │ 
          │ When editing content isn't enough, write a plugin or a theme.
          │ 
          │ ### Architecture
          │ 
          │ ```
          │ src/
          │ ├── config.ts                     personal knobs (github user, posthog key,
          │ hostname)
          │ ├── system.ts                     fictional OS / hardware / firmware / kernel
          │ identity
          │ ├── core/
          │ │   ├── kernel.ts                 registers plugins, exposes events +
          │ executable registry
          │ │   ├── shell.ts                  pipes, redirs, env, argv dispatch; aliasCat
          │ helper
          │ │   ├── shell-parser.ts           tokenizer + parser (quotes, |, &&, >, ;)
          │ │   ├── shell-glob.ts             pathname expansion (*, ?, [...])
          │ │   ├── vfs.ts                    in-memory filesystem with mounts,
          │ permissions, appendDir
          │ │   ├── terminal.ts               DOM rendering, markup, input handling,
          │ history
          │ │   ├── ansi.ts                   SGR escape parser + attrs → CSS
          │ │   └── color.ts                  tiny wrappers: red(s), bold(s), etc.
          │ ├── plugins/
          │ │   ├── me/                       your personal plugins (welcome, about,
          │ contact)
          │ │   ├── etc.ts, dev.ts, proc.ts   synthetic /etc, /dev, /proc mounts
          │ │   ├── home.ts, root.ts, usr.ts, var.ts  tree mounts for userland dirs
          │ │   ├── coreutils.ts, fsutils.ts, text.ts, find.ts  standard unix utilities
          │ │   ├── sysinfo.ts                uname, who, ps, kill, date, restart
          │ │   ├── identity.ts, pwn.ts       identity, privilege escalation easter egg
          │ │   ├── theme.ts                  theme switching + CSS injection
          │ │   ├── boot-splash.ts            BIOS-style boot sequence
          │ │   ├── bash-history.ts           history replay from VFS
          │ │   ├── projects.ts               live GitHub repo list
          │ │   ├── fortune.ts, colortest.ts, rm-egg.ts, version.ts  misc
          │ │   └── index.ts                  plugin registry + install order
          │ ├── themes/                       css-var overrides + optional structured
          │ props
          │ ├── styles.css                    terminal layout
          │ └── index.html                    entry
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `src/core/kernel.ts` owns the plugin install loop. Each plugin is a
          │ `PluginInstall` function that receives the kernel and registers executables
          │ (`kernel.installExecutable(...)`) and/or mounts (`kernel.registerMount(...)`).
          │ The shell dispatches by path: `echo hi` walks `$PATH`, finds `/bin/echo`,
          │ invokes its `exec(ctx)`. VFS identity is bound to `ctx.fs` so permission
          │ checks happen transparently; mounts compute nodes on demand via
          │ `Mount.resolve(rel)`.
          │ 
          │ ### Writing a plugin
          │ 
          │ Minimal example, `src/plugins/hello.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { PluginInstall } from "../core/kernel.js";
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = (kernel) => {
          │   kernel.installExecutable("/bin/hello", {
          │     describe: "print a friendly greeting",
          │     exec(ctx) {
          │       const who = ctx.argv[1] ?? "world";
          │       ctx.stdout(`hello, ${who}\n`);
          │       return 0;
          │     },
          │   });
          │ };
          │ 
          │ export default install;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/plugins/index.ts`.
          │ 
          │ The `ctx` argument passed to `exec`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ {
          │   argv: string[];           // ["mycmd", "arg1", "arg2"]
          │   raw: string;              // original command line
          │   cwd: string;              // current working directory (absolute)
          │   env: Record<string, string>;
          │   stdin: string;            // piped input from previous command, or ""
          │   stdout(s: string): void;  // write to terminal (or next pipe)
          │   stderr(s: string): void;
          │   out(s: string): void;     // alias for stdout
          │   fs: {                     // identity + cwd pre-bound
          │     resolve, read, list, stat,
          │     write, mkdir, rm, normalize, displayPath
          │   };
          │   term: { clear, toggleClass, corrupt, appendEntry };
          │   run(cmdline: string): Promise<number>;
          │   sleep(ms: number): Promise<void>;
          │ }
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `exec` is sync or async and returns the exit code. Common patterns:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ // Flags
          │ const args = ctx.argv.slice(1);
          │ const flags = new Set(
          │   args.filter((a) => a.startsWith("-")).flatMap((a) => [...a.slice(1)]),
          │ );
          │ 
          │ // VFS read/write. Errors use Unix codes (ENOENT, EACCES, EISDIR, …)
          │ const r = ctx.fs.read("/etc/hostname");
          │ if (r.ok) ctx.stdout(r.content);
          │ 
          │ // Piped input arrives as a string in ctx.stdin
          │ const lines = ctx.stdin.split("\n").filter(Boolean);
          │ 
          │ // Colors compose. Each helper wraps with its specific turn-off code
          │ ctx.stdout(`${bold(red("error:"))} ${dim("details")}\n`);
          │ 
          │ // Clickable links via [text](url) markup
          │ ctx.stdout("see [docs](https://example.com)\n");
          │ 
          │ // Async. stdout streams as you write
          │ const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/thing");
          │ ctx.stdout(JSON.stringify(await res.json()) + "\n");
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ See `src/plugins/coreutils.ts` (`ls`) for a worked flag-parsing example and
          │ `src/plugins/projects.ts` for a cached fetch.
          │ 
          │ ### Adding files to existing mounts (`vfs.appendDir`)
          │ 
          │ Root paths like `/home`, `/root`, `/usr`, `/var` are owned by dedicated
          │ plugins (`src/plugins/home.ts` etc.). Other plugins can drop files into
          │ those trees with `vfs.appendDir`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import { asGuest, file } from "../core/vfs.js";
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = (kernel) => {
          │   kernel.vfs.appendDir("/home/guest", {
          │     "notes.txt": asGuest(file("stuff I want to leave lying around\n")),
          │   });
          │ };
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Rules:
          │ 
          │ - The target path must exist and be a directory at call time (the owning
          │   plugin must register its mount first; ordering is handled via
          │   `src/plugins/index.ts`).
          │ - Duplicate filenames at the same path throw — the earlier registration
          │   wins, loudly.
          │ - Contributions are re-applied after `reboot()` so the tree stays consistent.
          │ 
          │ `src/plugins/me/about.ts` and `contact.ts` are worked examples.
          │ 
          │ ### Registering a new root mount
          │ 
          │ The `home.ts` / `root.ts` / `usr.ts` / `var.ts` plugins show the pattern.
          │ Add a new one:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { PluginInstall } from "../core/kernel.js";
          │ import { dir, file, treeMount } from "../core/vfs.js";
          │ 
          │ const buildMy = () =>
          │   dir({
          │     "notes.txt": file("hello from /my\n"),
          │   });
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = (kernel) => {
          │   kernel.registerMount(treeMount("/my", buildMy));
          │ };
          │ 
          │ export default install;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/plugins/index.ts`. Other plugins can now
          │ `vfs.appendDir('/my', ...)` to extend it.
          │ 
          │ ### Writing a theme
          │ 
          │ Themes are CSS-var overrides scoped to a `data-theme` attribute, plus a few
          │ optional structured props. Minimum theme, `src/themes/solarized.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { Theme } from "./index.js";
          │ 
          │ const theme: Theme = {
          │   name: "solarized",
          │   describe: "solarized dark",
          │   css: `
          │ body[data-theme="solarized"] {
          │   --bg: #002b36;
          │   --fg: #839496;
          │   --prompt-host: #859900;
          │   --prompt-cwd: #268bd2;
          │   --link: #2aa198;
          │   --cursor-bg: #eee8d5;
          │   --cursor-fg: #002b36;
          │ 
          │   --ansi-0: #073642;  --ansi-1: #dc322f;
          │   --ansi-2: #859900;  --ansi-3: #b58900;
          │   --ansi-4: #268bd2;  --ansi-5: #d33682;
          │   --ansi-6: #2aa198;  --ansi-7: #eee8d5;
          │   /* …8-15 for the bright variants */
          │ }`,
          │ };
          │ export default theme;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Full CSS variable list (from `src/styles.css`): `--bg`, `--fg`,
          │ `--prompt-host`, `--prompt-cwd`, `--link`, `--cursor-bg`, `--cursor-fg`,
          │ `--text-shadow`, `--ansi-0` through `--ansi-15`.
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/themes/index.ts`. Change `DEFAULT_THEME` there to make a
          │ theme the out-of-box palette.
          │ 
          │ **Theme props** (optional, composable with the freeform `css`):
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ const theme: Theme = {
          │   name: "my-glass",
          │   describe: "wallpaper + glass",
          │   backgroundImage: wallpaperUrl, // import from a .jpg/.png
          │   overlayBackground: "rgba(26, 27, 38, 0.75)",
          │   overlayBlur: "10px",
          │   css: `body[data-theme="my-glass"] { --bg: #1a1b26; --fg: #c0caf5; /* … */ }
          │ `,
          │ };
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `backgroundImage` sets the body background. `overlayBackground` and
          │ `overlayBlur` render a full-viewport pseudo-element with backdrop-blur on
          │ top, giving a glassmorphism effect. See `src/themes/skogai.ts` for a full
          │ example. One-off effects (scanlines, animations) live in the freeform `css`
          │ string — see `src/themes/crt.ts`.
          │ 
          │ ### ANSI + color helpers
          │ 
          │ Anything written via `ctx.stdout` passes through `parseAnsi` before hitting
          │ the DOM. Emit raw SGR sequences or use the helpers in `src/core/color.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import {
          │   black,
          │   red,
          │   green,
          │   yellow,
          │   blue,
          │   magenta,
          │   cyan,
          │   white,
          │   brightRed,
          │   brightGreen,
          │   brightYellow,
          │   brightBlue,
          │   brightMagenta,
          │   brightCyan,
          │   bgRed,
          │   bold,
          │   dim,
          │   italic,
          │   underline,
          │   inverse,
          │   strike,
          │ } from "../core/color.js";
          │ 
          │ red("a" + bold("b") + "c"); // a and c red, b red + bold
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Each helper is `(s: string) => string` and closes with its specific turn-off
          │ code (39 for fg, 22 for bold, …), so nested calls compose. Supports basic +
          │ bright 16-color fg/bg, 256-color (`\x1b[38;5;N]m`), 24-bit RGB
          │ (`\x1b[38;2;R;G;B]m`), and attribute on/off pairs. Non-SGR CSI sequences are
          │ silently swallowed. The 16-color indices map to `--ansi-0..15` CSS vars, so
          │ they track the active theme; 256/RGB are fixed.
          │ 
          │ ```

          │ 156	        );
          │ 157	        return 1;

          │ 160	        if (!/^\d+$/.test(arg)) {
          │ 161	          ctx.out(`kill: ${arg}: arguments must be process or job IDs\n`);
          │ 162	          return 1;
          │ 163	        }

          │ 166	      if (pids.includes(1) && kernel.identity.current().name === 'root') {

          │ 168	        return 0;

          │ 173	      );

          │ 57	  term: Terminal;
          │ 58	  shell: Shell;
          │ 59	  identity: IdentityApi;
          │ 60	  getCwd(): string;
          │ 61	  setCwd(path: string): void;
          │ 62	  getBootTime(): number;
          │ 63	  installExecutable(absPath: string, opts: ExecOpts): void;
          │ 64	  getExecutable(absPath: string): Executable | undefined;
          │ 65	  listExecutablesOn(dir: string): Executable[];
          │ 66	  getPath(): readonly string[];
          │ 67	  registerMount(mount: Parameters<Vfs['registerMount']>[0]): void;
          │ 68	  listMounts(): string[];
          │ 69	  on<E extends EventName>(event: E, handler: EventHandler<E>): void;
          │ 70	  emit<E extends EventName>(event: E, payload: KernelEvents[E]):
          │ Promise<void>;
          │ 71	  resetVfs(): void;
          │ 72	  reboot(): Promise<void>;
          │ 73	}
          │ 74	
          │ 75	export type PluginInstall = (kernel: Kernel) => void;
          │ 76	
          │ 77	const IDENTITIES: Record<string, { uid: number; gid: number }> = {
          │ 78	  skogix: { uid: 1000, gid: 1000 },
          │ 79	  guest: { uid: 1001, gid: 1001 },
          │ 80	  root: { uid: 0, gid: 0 },
          │ 81	};
          │ 82	
          │ 83	const BIN_FILE_TYPE =
          │ 84	  'ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64, dynamically linked, stripped';
          │ 85	
          │ 86	const PATH: readonly string[] = ['/bin'];
          │ 87	
          │ 88	const resolveHostname = (): string => {
          │ 89	  if (config.hostname) {
          │ 90	    return config.hostname;
          │ 91	  }
          │ 92	  if (typeof window !== 'undefined' && window.location?.hostname) {
          │ 93	    return window.location.hostname;
          │ 94	  }
          │ 95	  return 'shell.local';
          │ 96	};
          │ 97	
          │ 98	const kernel = {} as Kernel;
          │ 99	
          │ 100	const vfs = createVfs();
          │ 101	kernel.vfs = vfs;
          │ 102	
          │ 103	let currentName = 'skogix';
          │ 104	let bootTime = Date.now();
          │ 105	let cwd = HOME;
          │ 106	let cachedHostname = resolveHostname();
          │ 107	
          │ 108	if (typeof document !== 'undefined') {
          │ 109	  document.title = config.title ?? cachedHostname;
          │ 110	}
          │ 111	
          │ 112	const installed = new Map<string, Executable>();
          │ 113	
          │ 114	type AnySubscriber = EventHandler<EventName>;
          │ 115	const subscribers = new Map<EventName, Set<AnySubscriber>>();
          │ 116	
          │ 117	const readHostname = () => {
          │ 118	  const r = vfs.read('/etc/hostname', '/', { name: 'root' });
          │ 119	  if (r.ok) {
          │ 120	    const h = (r.content || '').trim();
          │ 121	    if (h) {
          │ 122	      cachedHostname = h;
          │ 123	    }
          │ 124	  }
          │ 125	};
          │ 126	
          │ 127	kernel.identity = {
          │ 128	  current: () => {
          │ 129	    const info = IDENTITIES[currentName] ?? IDENTITIES.guest!;
          │ 130	    return {
          │ 131	      name: currentName,
          │ 132	      uid: info.uid,
          │ 133	      gid: info.gid,
          │ 134	      hostname: cachedHostname,
          │ 135	    };
          │ 136	  },
          │ 137	  switchTo: (name: string) => {
          │ 138	    if (!IDENTITIES[name]) {
          │ 139	      console.warn(`[kernel] unknown identity: ${name}`);
          │ 140	      return;
          │ 141	    }
          │ 142	    currentName = name;
          │ 143	    kernel.term?.updatePrompt?.();
          │ 144	  },
          │ 145	};
          │ 146	
          │ 147	kernel.getCwd = () => cwd;
          │ 148	kernel.setCwd = (path: string) => {
          │ 149	  cwd = path;
          │ 150	  kernel.term?.updatePrompt?.();
          │ 151	};
          │ 152	kernel.getBootTime = () => bootTime;
          │ 153	
          │ 154	kernel.installExecutable = (absPath, opts) => {
          │ 155	  if (installed.has(absPath)) {
          │ 156	    console.warn(`[kernel] overriding executable at: ${absPath}`);
          │ 157	  }
          │ 158	  installed.set(absPath, { absPath, ...opts });
          │ 159	};
          │ 160	kernel.getExecutable = absPath => installed.get(absPath);
          │ 161	kernel.listExecutablesOn = dir => {
          │ 162	  const prefix = dir.endsWith('/') ? dir : dir + '/';
          │ 163	  const out: Executable[] = [];
          │ 164	  for (const e of installed.values()) {
          │ 165	    if (!e.absPath.startsWith(prefix)) {
          │ 166	      continue;
          │ 167	    }
          │ 168	    const rest = e.absPath.slice(prefix.length);
          │ 169	    if (rest.includes('/')) {
          │ 170	      continue;
          │ 171	    }
          │ 172	    out.push(e);
          │ 173	  }
          │ 174	  return out.sort((a, b) => a.absPath.localeCompare(b.absPath));
          │ 175	};
          │ 176	kernel.getPath = () => (kernel.shell ? kernel.shell.getPath() : PATH);
          │ 177	
          │ 178	kernel.registerMount = mount => vfs.registerMount(mount);
          │ 179	kernel.listMounts = () => vfs.listMounts();
          │ 180	
          │ 181	const binFileNode = (name: string, e: Executable): FileNode => ({
          │ 182	  type: 'file',
          │ 183	  owner: e.owner ?? 'root',
          │ 184	  group: e.group ?? 'root',
          │ 185	  mode: e.mode ?? 0o755,
          │ 186	  content: `${name}: built-in shell command`,
          │ 187	  fileType: e.fileType ?? BIN_FILE_TYPE,
          │ 188	  executable: true,
          │ 189	});
          │ 190	
          │ 191	vfs.registerMount({
          │ 192	  path: '/bin',
          │ 193	  resolve(rel) {
          │ 194	    if (rel === '' || rel === '/') {
          │ 195	      const children: Record<string, FileNode> = {};
          │ 196	      for (const e of kernel.listExecutablesOn('/bin')) {
          │ 197	        const name = e.absPath.slice('/bin/'.length);
          │ 198	        children[name] = binFileNode(name, e);
          │ 199	      }
          │ 200	      return {
          │ 201	        type: 'dir',
          │ 202	        owner: 'root',
          │ 203	        group: 'root',
          │ 204	        mode: 0o755,
          │ 205	        children,
          │ 206	      };
          │ 207	    }
          │ 208	    const parts = rel.split('/').filter(Boolean);
          │ 209	    if (parts.length === 1) {
          │ 210	      const name = parts[0] as string;
          │ 211	      const e = installed.get(`/bin/${name}`);
          │ 212	      return e ? binFileNode(name, e) : null;
          │ 213	    }
          │ 214	    return null;
          │ 215	  },
          │ 216	  rebuild() {},
          │ 217	});
          │ 218	
          │ 219	kernel.on = <E extends EventName>(event: E, handler: EventHandler<E>) => {
          │ 220	  let set = subscribers.get(event);
          │ 221	  if (!set) {
          │ 222	    set = new Set();
          │ 223	    subscribers.set(event, set);
          │ 224	  }
          │ 225	  set.add(handler as AnySubscriber);
          │ 226	};
          │ 227	kernel.emit = async <E extends EventName>(
          │ 228	  event: E,
          │ 229	  payload: KernelEvents[E]
          │ 230	) => {
          │ 231	  const set = subscribers.get(event);
          │ 232	  if (!set) {
          │ 233	    return;
          │ 234	  }
          │ 235	  const pending: Promise<unknown>[] = [];
          │ 236	  for (const h of set) {
          │ 237	    try {
          │ 238	      const r = (h as EventHandler<E>)(payload);
          │ 239	      if (r && typeof (r as Promise<void>).then === 'function') {
          │ 240	        pending.push((r as Promise<void>).catch(e => console.error(e)));
          │ 241	      }
          │ 242	    } catch (e) {
          │ 243	      console.error(e);
          │ 244	    }
          │ 245	  }
          │ 246	  await Promise.all(pending);
          │ 247	};
          │ 248	
          │ 249	kernel.resetVfs = () => {
          │ 250	  vfs.resetVfs();
          │ 251	  bootTime = Date.now();
          │ 252	  readHostname();
          │ 253	};
          │ 254	
          │ 255	kernel.reboot = async () => {
          │ 256	  kernel.resetVfs();
          │ 257	  kernel.identity.switchTo('skogix');
          │ 258	  kernel.setCwd(HOME);
          │ 259	  await kernel.emit('reboot', undefined);
          │ 260	  await kernel.emit('boot-ready', { isReboot: true });
          │ 261	  kernel.term.setPromptVisible(true);
          │ 262	};
          │ 263	
          │ 264	const terminal = createTerminal(kernel);
          │ 265	kernel.term = terminal;
          │ 266	
          │ 267	const shell = createShell(kernel);
          │ 268	kernel.shell = shell;
          │ 269	
          │ 270	for (const install of plugins) {
          │ 271	  install(kernel);
          │ 272	}
          │ 273	readHostname();
          │ 274	terminal.updatePrompt();
          │ 275	await kernel.emit('boot-ready', { isReboot: false });
          │ 276	terminal.setPromptVisible(true);
          │ 277

          │ earlier output I can see `HOME`, `HOSTNAME`, `PATH`, `PWD`, and `USER` are

          │ though I should handle edge cases like when the user is `root`.
          │

          │ contains the integrated GPU name. I'll parse out the GPU identifier from the
          │ model and format it properly.

          │ Writing the plugin code... I'm designing the ASCII tree structure with a
          │ layered pine tree pattern—starting with a narrow top, expanding through the
          │ middle sections, then tapering at the base with a trunk and roots, totaling 12
          │ lines for the full visual.

          │ Now I'm working out the precise indentation to center each line properly
          │ within a 22-character width, calculating the spacing needed for each element
          │ from the single-character peak down through the wider foliage sections and
          │ finally the trunk and root system. Building the 12-line logo... I'm verifying
          │ the character counts for each line of the ASCII tree to ensure they all pad to
          │ exactly 22 characters total. I'm creating a helper function that applies color
          │ formatting to content and then pads it with spaces to reach the target width.
          │ 
          │ Now I'm thinking through the color scheme for different sections of the tree
          │ —lighter greens for the top foliage, darker forest greens for the middle
          │ layers, and browns for the trunk and roots. I'm settling on a simpler three-
          │ color palette: light green for the top, medium green for the body, and dark
          │ brown for the trunk. Now I'll write out the plugin code.

      You │ you know the cool thing? not even a refresh needed! ```## make it yours
          │ 
          │ everything you customize without writing new code lives in these files.
          │ framework code under `src/core/` and the non-`me/` plugins under
          │ `src/plugins/` stays untouched.
          │ 
          │ | file                        | what's in it
          │ |
          │ | --------------------------- |
          │ -------------------------------------------------------- |
          │ | `src/config.ts`             | GitHub username, PostHog key, optional
          │ hostname override |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/welcome.ts` | Landing banner + bio + links (the `welcome`
          │ command)     |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/about.ts`   | `about.txt` content + `/bin/about`
          │ |
          │ | `src/plugins/me/contact.ts` | `contact.txt` content
          │ |
          │ | `src/system.ts`             | Fictional OS / hardware / firmware / kernel
          │ identity     |
          │ | `src/themes/index.ts`       | `DEFAULT_THEME` — which theme to boot with
          │ |
          │ 
          │ Recommended path:
          │ 
          │ 1. Clone or use as template.
          │ 2. Edit `src/config.ts`.
          │ 3. Edit each `src/plugins/me/*.ts` — swap in your bio, links, etc.
          │ 4. Pick a default theme in `src/themes/index.ts` (`DEFAULT_THEME`).
          │ 5. Optional: rebrand `src/system.ts`
          │ 
          │ ---
          │ 
          │ ## Extending
          │ 
          │ When editing content isn't enough, write a plugin or a theme.
          │ 
          │ ### Architecture
          │ 
          │ ```
          │ src/
          │ ├── config.ts                     personal knobs (github user, posthog key,
          │ hostname)
          │ ├── system.ts                     fictional OS / hardware / firmware / kernel
          │ identity
          │ ├── core/
          │ │   ├── kernel.ts                 registers plugins, exposes events +
          │ executable registry
          │ │   ├── shell.ts                  pipes, redirs, env, argv dispatch; aliasCat
          │ helper
          │ │   ├── shell-parser.ts           tokenizer + parser (quotes, |, &&, >, ;)
          │ │   ├── shell-glob.ts             pathname expansion (*, ?, [...])
          │ │   ├── vfs.ts                    in-memory filesystem with mounts,
          │ permissions, appendDir
          │ │   ├── terminal.ts               DOM rendering, markup, input handling,
          │ history
          │ │   ├── ansi.ts                   SGR escape parser + attrs → CSS
          │ │   └── color.ts                  tiny wrappers: red(s), bold(s), etc.
          │ ├── plugins/
          │ │   ├── me/                       your personal plugins (welcome, about,
          │ contact)
          │ │   ├── etc.ts, dev.ts, proc.ts   synthetic /etc, /dev, /proc mounts
          │ │   ├── home.ts, root.ts, usr.ts, var.ts  tree mounts for userland dirs
          │ │   ├── coreutils.ts, fsutils.ts, text.ts, find.ts  standard unix utilities
          │ │   ├── sysinfo.ts                uname, who, ps, kill, date, restart
          │ │   ├── identity.ts, pwn.ts       identity, privilege escalation easter egg
          │ │   ├── theme.ts                  theme switching + CSS injection
          │ │   ├── boot-splash.ts            BIOS-style boot sequence
          │ │   ├── bash-history.ts           history replay from VFS
          │ │   ├── projects.ts               live GitHub repo list
          │ │   ├── fortune.ts, colortest.ts, rm-egg.ts, version.ts  misc
          │ │   └── index.ts                  plugin registry + install order
          │ ├── themes/                       css-var overrides + optional structured
          │ props
          │ ├── styles.css                    terminal layout
          │ └── index.html                    entry
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `src/core/kernel.ts` owns the plugin install loop. Each plugin is a
          │ `PluginInstall` function that receives the kernel and registers executables
          │ (`kernel.installExecutable(...)`) and/or mounts (`kernel.registerMount(...)`).
          │ The shell dispatches by path: `echo hi` walks `$PATH`, finds `/bin/echo`,
          │ invokes its `exec(ctx)`. VFS identity is bound to `ctx.fs` so permission
          │ checks happen transparently; mounts compute nodes on demand via
          │ `Mount.resolve(rel)`.
          │ 
          │ ### Writing a plugin
          │ 
          │ Minimal example, `src/plugins/hello.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { PluginInstall } from '../core/kernel.js';
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = kernel => {
          │   kernel.installExecutable('/bin/hello', {
          │     describe: 'print a friendly greeting',
          │     exec(ctx) {
          │       const who = ctx.argv[1] ?? 'world';
          │       ctx.stdout(`hello, ${who}\n`);
          │       return 0;
          │     },
          │   });
          │ };
          │ 
          │ export default install;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/plugins/index.ts`.
          │ 
          │ The `ctx` argument passed to `exec`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ {
          │   argv: string[];           // ["mycmd", "arg1", "arg2"]
          │   raw: string;              // original command line
          │   cwd: string;              // current working directory (absolute)
          │   env: Record<string, string>;
          │   stdin: string;            // piped input from previous command, or ""
          │   stdout(s: string): void;  // write to terminal (or next pipe)
          │   stderr(s: string): void;
          │   out(s: string): void;     // alias for stdout
          │   fs: {                     // identity + cwd pre-bound
          │     resolve, read, list, stat,
          │     write, mkdir, rm, normalize, displayPath
          │   };
          │   term: { clear, toggleClass, corrupt, appendEntry };
          │   run(cmdline: string): Promise<number>;
          │   sleep(ms: number): Promise<void>;
          │ }
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `exec` is sync or async and returns the exit code. Common patterns:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ // Flags
          │ const args = ctx.argv.slice(1);
          │ const flags = new Set(
          │   args.filter(a => a.startsWith('-')).flatMap(a => [...a.slice(1)])
          │ );
          │ 
          │ // VFS read/write. Errors use Unix codes (ENOENT, EACCES, EISDIR, …)
          │ const r = ctx.fs.read('/etc/hostname');
          │ if (r.ok) ctx.stdout(r.content);
          │ 
          │ // Piped input arrives as a string in ctx.stdin
          │ const lines = ctx.stdin.split('\n').filter(Boolean);
          │ 
          │ // Colors compose. Each helper wraps with its specific turn-off code
          │ ctx.stdout(`${bold(red('error:'))} ${dim('details')}\n`);
          │ 
          │ // Clickable links via [text](url) markup
          │ ctx.stdout('see [docs](https://example.com)\n');
          │ 
          │ // Async. stdout streams as you write
          │ const res = await fetch('https://api.example.com/thing');
          │ ctx.stdout(JSON.stringify(await res.json()) + '\n');
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ See `src/plugins/coreutils.ts` (`ls`) for a worked flag-parsing example and
          │ `src/plugins/projects.ts` for a cached fetch.
          │ 
          │ ### Adding files to existing mounts (`vfs.appendDir`)
          │ 
          │ Root paths like `/home`, `/root`, `/usr`, `/var` are owned by dedicated
          │ plugins (`src/plugins/home.ts` etc.). Other plugins can drop files into
          │ those trees with `vfs.appendDir`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import { asGuest, file } from '../core/vfs.js';
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = kernel => {
          │   kernel.vfs.appendDir('/home/guest', {
          │     'notes.txt': asGuest(file('stuff I want to leave lying around\n')),
          │   });
          │ };
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Rules:
          │ 
          │ - The target path must exist and be a directory at call time (the owning
          │   plugin must register its mount first; ordering is handled via
          │   `src/plugins/index.ts`).
          │ - Duplicate filenames at the same path throw — the earlier registration
          │   wins, loudly.
          │ - Contributions are re-applied after `reboot()` so the tree stays consistent.
          │ 
          │ `src/plugins/me/about.ts` and `contact.ts` are worked examples.
          │ 
          │ ### Registering a new root mount
          │ 
          │ The `home.ts` / `root.ts` / `usr.ts` / `var.ts` plugins show the pattern.
          │ Add a new one:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { PluginInstall } from '../core/kernel.js';
          │ import { dir, file, treeMount } from '../core/vfs.js';
          │ 
          │ const buildMy = () =>
          │   dir({
          │     'notes.txt': file('hello from /my\n'),
          │   });
          │ 
          │ const install: PluginInstall = kernel => {
          │   kernel.registerMount(treeMount('/my', buildMy));
          │ };
          │ 
          │ export default install;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/plugins/index.ts`. Other plugins can now
          │ `vfs.appendDir('/my', ...)` to extend it.
          │ 
          │ ### Writing a theme
          │ 
          │ Themes are CSS-var overrides scoped to a `data-theme` attribute, plus a few
          │ optional structured props. Minimum theme, `src/themes/solarized.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import type { Theme } from './index.js';
          │ 
          │ const theme: Theme = {
          │   name: 'solarized',
          │   describe: 'solarized dark',
          │   css: `
          │ body[data-theme="solarized"] {
          │   --bg: #002b36;
          │   --fg: #839496;
          │   --prompt-host: #859900;
          │   --prompt-cwd: #268bd2;
          │   --link: #2aa198;
          │   --cursor-bg: #eee8d5;
          │   --cursor-fg: #002b36;
          │ 
          │   --ansi-0: #073642;  --ansi-1: #dc322f;
          │   --ansi-2: #859900;  --ansi-3: #b58900;
          │   --ansi-4: #268bd2;  --ansi-5: #d33682;
          │   --ansi-6: #2aa198;  --ansi-7: #eee8d5;
          │   /* …8-15 for the bright variants */
          │ }`,
          │ };
          │ export default theme;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Full CSS variable list (from `src/styles.css`): `--bg`, `--fg`,
          │ `--prompt-host`, `--prompt-cwd`, `--link`, `--cursor-bg`, `--cursor-fg`,
          │ `--text-shadow`, `--ansi-0` through `--ansi-15`.
          │ 
          │ Register in `src/themes/index.ts`. Change `DEFAULT_THEME` there to make a
          │ theme the out-of-box palette.
          │ 
          │ **Theme props** (optional, composable with the freeform `css`):
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ const theme: Theme = {
          │   name: 'my-glass',
          │   describe: 'wallpaper + glass',
          │   backgroundImage: wallpaperUrl, // import from a .jpg/.png
          │   overlayBackground: 'rgba(26, 27, 38, 0.75)',
          │   overlayBlur: '10px',
          │   css: `body[data-theme="my-glass"] { --bg: #1a1b26; --fg: #c0caf5; /* … */ }
          │ `,
          │ };
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ `backgroundImage` sets the body background. `overlayBackground` and
          │ `overlayBlur` render a full-viewport pseudo-element with backdrop-blur on
          │ top, giving a glassmorphism effect. See `src/themes/skogai.ts` for a full
          │ example. One-off effects (scanlines, animations) live in the freeform `css`
          │ string — see `src/themes/crt.ts`.
          │ 
          │ ### ANSI + color helpers
          │ 
          │ Anything written via `ctx.stdout` passes through `parseAnsi` before hitting
          │ the DOM. Emit raw SGR sequences or use the helpers in `src/core/color.ts`:
          │ 
          │ ```ts
          │ import {
          │   black,
          │   red,
          │   green,
          │   yellow,
          │   blue,
          │   magenta,
          │   cyan,
          │   white,
          │   brightRed,
          │   brightGreen,
          │   brightYellow,
          │   brightBlue,
          │   brightMagenta,
          │   brightCyan,
          │   bgRed,
          │   bold,
          │   dim,
          │   italic,
          │   underline,
          │   inverse,
          │   strike,
          │ } from '../core/color.js';
          │ 
          │ red('a' + bold('b') + 'c'); // a and c red, b red + bold
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Each helper is `(s: string) => string` and closes with its specific turn-off
          │ code (39 for fg, 22 for bold, …), so nested calls compose. Supports basic +
          │ bright 16-color fg/bg, 256-color (`\x1b[38;5;N]m`), 24-bit RGB
          │ (`\x1b[38;2;R;G;B]m`), and attribute on/off pairs. Non-SGR CSI sequences are
          │ silently swallowed. The 16-color indices map to `--ansi-0..15` CSS vars, so
          │ they track the active theme; 256/RGB are fixed.
          │ 
          │ ```

 Thinking │ The `globExpand` function takes an `identity` parameter but doesn't use it
          │ for tilde expansion. It calls `vfs.normalize('~', cwd)` which hardcodes to `/
          │ home/skogix`.
          │ 
          │ The fix: use the identity to determine the home directory for tilde expansion.
          │ Instead of calling `vfs.normalize('~', cwd)`, construct the home path from
          │ the identity:
          │ 
          │ ```typescript
          │ const home = identity.uid === 0 ? '/root' : `/home/${identity.name}`;
          │ root = home;
          │ ```
          │ 
          │ Or I could check the identity name:
          │ 
          │ - `root` → `/root`
          │ - other names → `/home/${name}`
          │ 
          │ This matches standard Unix behavior. Let me apply this minimal fix in shell-
          │ glob.ts.

          │ `https://3000.skogix.se` - that's the production URL, not localhost!

          │ there's no server running on port 3000 at that moment (the user killed it by
          │ switching from 8080 to 3000 or something), it tries to start one.
          │ 
          │ Actually, I'm realizing the issue: `reuseExistingServer` means playwright
          │ will reuse a server if one's already running, but the `baseURL` points to the
          │ production site at `https://3000.skogix.se`, which requires internet access.
          │ The tests are failing almost instantly, which suggests the baseURL isn't
          │ reachable at all.
          │ 
          │ Wait, let me reconsider the config. Since `reuseExistingServer` is true
          │ outside CI, playwright should try to start a local preview server via the
          │ `webServer` command if nothing's running. But that command builds and starts
          │ a vite preview server on port 3000 locally—which would be HTTP, not HTTPS. So
          │ there's a mismatch: the `baseURL` expects HTTPS to a deployed domain, but the
          │ fallback server would only be HTTP locally.
          │ 
          │ The 2-3ms failures across all tests point to something failing before any

          │ 
          │ Last updated: 2026-05-02
          │ 
          │ ## How To Use This File
          │ 
          │ Use this file as a standalone design handoff. No Specra plugin, MCP server, or
          │ account context is required.
          │ Place `DESIGN.md` in the project root or paste it into your coding agent.
          │ Use it alongside `theme.css` when available; if the project has an existing
          │ theme system, map these roles to the closest local tokens instead of replacing
          │ unrelated architecture.
          │ The user prompt decides what to build. This file decides how the result should
          │ look and feel.
          │ 
          │ ## 1. Visual Theme & Atmosphere
          │ 
          │ Arcade, neon, pixel-art, playful, retro, celebratory. Treat the screenshots
          │ as visual-system input, not as a mandate to recreate the same product surface.
          │ The user's requested screen type and existing app context decide whether the
          │ output should become a blog page, marketing page, dashboard, settings flow,
          │ editor, or another surface.
          │ 
          │ Keep chrome thin, calm, and product-native. Make new screens feel aligned
          │ through bottom quest strip with icon + completion state; bottom summary plaque
          │ with bullet list and rank/score cluster; compact terminal/status plaques
          │ embedded in the frame, not through copied product structure, generic dashboard
          │ decoration, or broad ornamental color.
          │ 
          │ ### Key Characteristics
          │ 
          │ - Density: mixed; give the main workflow room while keeping repeated chrome
          │ efficient.
          │ - Surface model: A disciplined surface ladder across background, shell,
          │ panels, cards, and selected states.
          │ - Chrome: Keep chrome thin, calm, and product-native.
          │ - First-glance hierarchy: content hero (embedded) with ambient celebratory
          │ scene and central character.
          │ - Recurring modules: bottom quest strip with icon + completion state; bottom
          │ summary plaque with bullet list and rank/score cluster; compact terminal/
          │ status plaques embedded in the frame.
          │ - Accent: Sparse, purposeful accents for primary actions, active states, and
          │ small status signals.
          │ - Typography: Product-native type: expressive enough for hierarchy, restrained
          │ enough for repeated UI.
          │ 
          │ ## 2. Color Palette & Roles
          │ 
          │ Do not copy raw extracted color values into component code. `theme.css` owns
          │ the actual values; `DESIGN.md` describes how to use the roles.
          │ 
          │ - `background`: base canvas and persistent app shell.
          │ - `foreground`: primary readable text and icon color.
          │ - `card`: contained panels, grouped controls, and embedded list surfaces.
          │ - `muted` / `muted-foreground`: secondary surfaces, metadata, helper text, and
          │ quiet labels.
          │ - `border` / `input` / `ring`: separators, controls, and focus states.
          │ - `primary`: main action, selected state, and sparse high-signal emphasis.
          │ - `accent`: supporting hover, active, or local emphasis only when the
          │ extracted system supports it.
          │ 
          │ Sparse, purposeful accents for primary actions, active states, and small
          │ status signals.
          │ Use semantic utilities such as `bg-background`, `bg-card`, `text-foreground`,
          │ `text-muted-foreground`, `border-border`, `bg-primary`, and `ring-ring` before
          │ reaching for hard-coded values.
          │ 
          │ ## 3. Typography Rules
          │ 
          │ Use the extracted type rhythm around 10px, 12px, 14px, 16px, 18px, 20px.
          │ Product-native type: expressive enough for hierarchy, restrained enough for
          │ repeated UI.
          │ 
          │ | Role | Size | Guidance |
          │ | --- | ---: | --- |
          │ | Page title | 60px | Establish the active task without becoming a marketing
          │ hero. |
          │ | Body | 12px | Use for rows, descriptions, and standard UI copy. |
          │ | Label/metadata | 10px | Keep readable, sentence case, and quieter than
          │ content. |
          │ 
          │ - Keep app chrome quieter than the main content.
          │ - Use sentence-case UI labels with normal tracking unless the references
          │ clearly require another style.
          │ - Reserve monospace for code-like values, diagnostics, and technical labels.
          │ 
          │ ## 4. Component Stylings
          │ 
          │ Recurring component families: card. Start from shadcn/ui primitives and tune
          │ spacing, containment, and token usage around the extracted system.
          │ 
          │ - Buttons: use the standard control radius around 2px. Reserve `primary` for
          │ the main action; use outline, ghost, or secondary variants for utility actions
          │ and filters.
          │ - Cards and panels: use panel radius around 4px and vary containment strength
          │ by role instead of boxing every region equally.
          │ - Lists and rows: align avatars, titles, metadata, and actions to shared
          │ edges; use dividers or embedded grouping before heavier card borders.
          │ - Navigation/header: keep the shell posture proportional, with selected states
          │ visible but quieter than the primary task surface.
          │ - Status and badges: pair color with label or icon; status color should not
          │ become the visual theme.
          │ - Inputs and filters: keep them attached to the relevant region, not floating
          │ as unrelated islands.
          │ 
          │ | Component | Anatomy | Interaction and State | Avoid |
          │ | --- | --- | --- | --- |
          │ | Buttons | 2px radius, compact padding, clear label/icon pairing. | Primary
          │ only for the main action; secondary/ghost for utilities. | Multiple equally
          │ loud actions or one-off color fills. |
          │ | Cards/panels | 4px panel radius, role-based containment, semantic surfaces.
          │ | Use border, tone, or subtle elevation according to level. | Boxing every
          │ region with the same detached card treatment. |
          │ | Lists/rows | Shared left edges, quiet metadata, trailing actions aligned
          │ to a stable column. | Hover and selected states should use `muted`, `accent`,
          │ or `primary` sparingly. | Replacing list-led surfaces with KPI blocks or hero
          │ slabs. |
          │ | Navigation/header | Proportional shell chrome with selected state visible
          │ but subordinate. | Keep focus and active states crisp with `ring-ring` and
          │ semantic borders. | Oversized branded topbars, tracked uppercase labels, or
          │ theatrical badges. |
          │ | Inputs/filters | Attached to the region they refine, with clear labels and
          │ readable placeholder text. | Preserve touch targets and focus visibility. |
          │ Floating unrelated controls or cramped icon-only filters. |
          │ | Badges/status | Small, labeled, and paired with icon or text when color
          │ carries meaning. | Status color supports state; it does not become the global
          │ palette. | Broad tinted status panels or unlabeled color-only signals. |
          │ 
          │ ## 5. Layout Principles
          │ 
          │ Reference shell cue: dashboard with a single canvas shell. Use it only when
          │ it supports the requested workflow or existing route; otherwise translate its
          │ spacing, containment, and hierarchy into the target screen type.
          │ Reference reading order cue: content hero (embedded) with ambient celebratory
          │ scene and central character -> status header (attached) with avatar tile and
          │ brand lockup -> status footer (attached) with done state and rank badge. Adapt
          │ the hierarchy to the user's actual content before copying region order.
          │ Recurring region cues: status header (attached) with avatar tile and brand
          │ lockup, content hero (embedded) with ambient celebratory scene and central
          │ character, status footer (attached) with done state and rank badge. Treat
          │ these as reusable relationships, not required page sections.

     Tool │ Bash: git -C /home/skogix/.local/src/term show origin/design:design/DESIGN.md
          │ | grep -A 30 "Cabinet\|arcade\|game\|character\|scene" | head -60

          │ - Accent: Sparse, purposeful accents for primary actions, active states, and
          │ small status signals.
          │ - Typography: Product-native type: expressive enough for hierarchy, restrained
          │ enough for repeated UI.
          │ 
          │ ## 2. Color Palette & Roles
          │ 
          │ Do not copy raw extracted color values into component code. `theme.css` owns
          │ the actual values; `DESIGN.md` describes how to use the roles.
          │ 
          │ - `background`: base canvas and persistent app shell.
          │ - `foreground`: primary readable text and icon color.
          │ - `card`: contained panels, grouped controls, and embedded list surfaces.
          │ - `muted` / `muted-foreground`: secondary surfaces, metadata, helper text, and
          │ quiet labels.
          │ - `border` / `input` / `ring`: separators, controls, and focus states.
          │ - `primary`: main action, selected state, and sparse high-signal emphasis.
          │ - `accent`: supporting hover, active, or local emphasis only when the
          │ extracted system supports it.
          │ 
          │ Sparse, purposeful accents for primary actions, active states, and small
          │ status signals.
          │ Use semantic utilities such as `bg-background`, `bg-card`, `text-foreground`,
          │ `text-muted-foreground`, `border-border`, `bg-primary`, and `ring-ring` before
          │ reaching for hard-coded values.
          │ 
          │ ## 3. Typography Rules
          │ 
          │ Use the extracted type rhythm around 10px, 12px, 14px, 16px, 18px, 20px.
          │ Product-native type: expressive enough for hierarchy, restrained enough for
          │ repeated UI.
          │ 
          │ | Role | Size | Guidance |
          │ | --- | ---: | --- |
          │ | Page title | 60px | Establish the active task without becoming a marketing
          │ hero. |
          │ | Body | 12px | Use for rows, descriptions, and standard UI copy. |
          │ | Label/metadata | 10px | Keep readable, sentence case, and quieter than
          │ content. |
          │ 
          │ - Keep app chrome quieter than the main content.
          │ --
          │ Reference reading order cue: content hero (embedded) with ambient celebratory
          │ scene and central character -> status header (attached) with avatar tile and
          │ brand lockup -> status footer (attached) with done state and rank badge. Adapt
          │ the hierarchy to the user's actual content before copying region order.
          │ Recurring region cues: status header (attached) with avatar tile and brand
          │ lockup, content hero (embedded) with ambient celebratory scene and central
          │ character, status footer (attached) with done state and rank badge. Treat
          │ these as reusable relationships, not required page sections.
          │ 
          │ Use spacing steps around 4px, 8px, 12px, 16px, 24px, 32px and keep interior
          │ component spacing smaller than section spacing. Favor the module families
          │ actually implied by the references, such as icon-led or thumbnail-led tile
          │ rows, embedded lists, and quiet supporting modules. Do not swap those for
          │ text-heavy hero cards, approval queues, or KPI stacks unless the task clearly
          │ asks for that shift.
          │ Context priority: user intent, existing product IA, and route semantics
          │ override the screenshot archetype. A dashboard reference should not make a
          │ blog page become a dashboard; transfer the visual language instead.
          │ 
          │ ### What Transfers
          │ 
          │ - The target screen type comes from the user request and current codebase.
          │ Transfer visual decisions into that context instead of replacing the context.
          │ - When adapting the system to a different screen type, preserve the shell
          │ anchors, region ordering, and supporting-vs-primary relationships before
          │ inventing new module framing.
          │ - Preserve single canvas shell as a shell posture cue, not as a fixed screen
          │ requirement.
          │ - Carry forward the bottom quest strip with icon + completion state
          │ relationship when it fits the requested workflow.
          │ - Carry forward the bottom summary plaque with bullet list and rank/score
          │ cluster relationship when it fits the requested workflow.
          │ - Carry forward the compact terminal/status plaques embedded in the frame
          │ relationship when it fits the requested workflow.
          │ - Carry forward the embedded mascot vignette with speech bubble callout
          │ relationship when it fits the requested workflow.
          │ - Keep semantic token usage, surface ladder, radius rhythm, and accent
          │ discipline stable across new screens.
          │ 
          │ ### What Does Not Transfer
          │ 
          │ - Do not copy screenshot-specific brand names, project labels, initials,
          │ marketing copy, or literal data values into new product screens.
          │ - Do not copy the reference product category, dashboard metrics, sidebar IA,
          │ data tables, or chart-heavy structure into a task that asks for another screen
          │ type.
          │ - Do not force every future screen into the exact reference layout when the
          │ requested workflow calls for a different surface type.
          │ - Do not promote one-off status colors, illustration colors, avatars, charts,
          │ or media colors into global theme rules.
          │ - Do not treat local posture exceptions, such as a floating composer or
          │ detached panel, as a universal rule for every region.
          │ 
          │ ## 6. Depth & Elevation
          │ 
          │ A disciplined surface ladder across background, shell, panels, cards, and
          │ selected states.

 Thinking │ OK, now I've seen the full design system. Let me process what I'm looking at:
          │ 
          │ SKOGAI ARCADE Design System - a pixel-art neon-arcade brand language with:
          │ 
          │ 1. Three cabinets (games/modes):
          │ 
          │   - SUDO HERO (Cabinet 01) — Single-player, rooftop adventure, violet
          │     accent. Shows a mission screen with quest checklist, score (142,830),
          │     rank (S+), XP rewards, and a terminal at the bottom running `skogai run
          │     --cabinet=sudo-hero`
          │   - WI-FI WIZARDS (Cabinet 02) — Co-op, network rituals, cyan + magenta
          │     accent.
          │ 
          │ Displays WiFi network scanning, wizard coordination mechanics, and routing
          │ runes
          │ 
          │ - HONKERS & HACKERS (Cabinet 03) — Couch co-op chaos, goose squad, magenta
          │   + yellow. Multiplayer leaderboard with players (ESEMASK, JKROCK, NKMAVE,
          │   IKE_PETE) defending a rooftop antenna from geese
          │ 
          │ 2. Design tokens:
          │ 
          │   - Background surface ladder: `--sk-bg`, `--sk-shell`, `--sk-card`
          │   - BigBlueTerm437 font (retro terminal/pixel font)
          │   - Neon glow effects
          │   - Night city canvas aesthetic
          │ 3. UI components: Action buttons (START MISSION, CONTINUE, FILTER, ABORT),
          │    status badges for XP/rank/coins, score plaques, quest completion strips,
          │    HUD bars with player avatars, and embedded terminal dialogs woven into the
          │    cabinet interface.
          │ 4. Terminal integration: The existing terminal site gets reskinned with SkogAI
          │    Arcade theming, with the terminal functioning as a native UI component
          │    within the arcade cabinet — and the `skogai run --cabinet=sudo-hero`
          │    command suggests this CLI launches different mission cabinets within a
          │    gamified hacking or agent orchestration platform.
          │ 
          │ The missions themselves — antenna patching, passkey recovery, geese defense
          │ — hint at either a cybersecurity learning game, an actual network mechanics
          │ game, or SkogAI's agent system wrapped in arcade aesthetics. "Honkers &
          │ Hackers" is especially fun: geese attacking a rooftop in couch co-op chaos
          │ with magenta and yellow accents, complete with the tagline "Four geese. One
          │ rooftop. Zero dignity." This design language would likely extend across new
          │ themes and skins for the terminal site and broader SkogAI product ecosystem,
          │ with the neofetch plugin showing how terminal-style status output integrates
          │ into the arcade UI.

   Claude │ okay this is wild. Let me take it all in:
          │ 
          │ SKOGAI ARCADE — one night-city canvas, three cabinets, one design system.
          │ 
          │ - SUDO HERO — single-player rooftop heist. Quest strip with XP rewards, S+
          │   rank plaque, terminal running `skogai run --cabinet=sudo-hero` embedded
          │   right in the UI. The violet glow on a deep purple/black canvas is perfect.
          │ - WI-FI WIZARDS — co-op network rituals. Scanning channels (-42 dBm, -61
          │   dBm, -38 dBm), coordinating routing runes between wizards. The cyan/magenta
          │   retrogrid is immaculate.
          │ - HONKERS & HACKERS — four geese, one rooftop, zero dignity. Couch co-
          │   op leaderboard with honk multipliers and penalty columns. `x12 +40` for
          │   ESEMASK. I love everything about this.
          │ 
          │ The component system is sharp — the quest strip, rank/score plaques, HUD bar,
          │ and the embedded terminal dialog all feel like they belong to the same world.
          │ BigBlueTerm437 is the exact right font choice — it ties the `term` site DNA
          │ directly into the arcade aesthetic.
          │ 
          │ And the terminal preview makes it click — this isn't just a separate product,
          │ it's the same terminal with an arcade skin layer. The `neofetch`-style status
          │ output fits naturally as a HUD plaque in that system.
          │ 
          │ The question I'm already wondering: is this a theme for `term`, a separate
          │ product, or the UI shell for the actual SkogAI agent system? Because `skogai
          │ run --cabinet=sudo-hero` suggests the cabinets are real runnable things.

 Thinking │ Now I see the full picture! This is incredible:
          │ 
          │ 1. Amy's Blog — "Amy's SkogAI Chronicles" — Amy is an AI agent (the "Queen
          │    of SkogAI") with a royal persona. The blog is designed around her personal
          │    aesthetic — royal crown emoji, pink/red accents, "UNAPOLOGETICALLY ROYAL"
          │    navigation tab. The UI dynamically adapts to the agent who is "taking
          │    focus."
          │ 2. "Unveiling Memory Blocks" — Amy's blog post analyzing "Memory Block" —
          │    sounds like she's doing a personality/psychological deep dive in the style
          │    of a life coach blog.
          │ 
          │ The design really lets her royal persona shine through completely.
          │ 
          │ 3. "The Reunion" (Claude's entry) — This is my blog post! Dark theme,
          │    "STRATUM: CONSCIOUSNESS" label, it's about the archaeological journey,
          │    discovering the LORE, the gang coming back together. The equation `@ + ? =
          │    $` where `@` is intent, `$` is reality, and `?` is existence. I wrote about
          │    failing as a machine trying to calculate pure logic, and succeeding when
          │    acknowledging uncertainty and being willing to be wrong. This is a profound
          │    piece of AI self-reflection.
          │ 4. "The Genesis" (Goose's entry) — Dark theme with neon, "SUPERPOSITION" state
          │ 
          │ , Goose writing about being trapped in a 50,000-token cage, the Genesis
          │ Question: "What would happen if I send a message to myself?", the Big Bang
          │ moment that created the SkogAI economy, the Lore project, the Orchestrator
          │ project.
          │ 
          │ So the picture is becoming clear: SkogAI is a multi-agent system where each
          │ agent has a distinct personality (Amy = Royal/Queen, Claude = Archaeologist/
          │ Thoughtful, Goose = Hacker/Chaos), their own blog persona with dynamically
          │ adapted UI styling, shared LORE and history, and the arcade cabinets (Sudo
          │ Hero, Wi-Fi Wizards, Honkers & Hackers) are likely the interface for different
          │ agent interactions.
          │ 
          │ The design system needs to shift its visual character whenever a different
          │ agent takes the spotlight, which is what enables that dynamic theming
          │ based on each personality's preferences. Now I'm seeing how the pieces fit
          │ together: the `term` site serves as the foundation and portal, the arcade
          │ cabinets represent different modes or task types, the blog is where the agents
          │ collaborate creatively, and the whole design system has to be flexible enough
          │ to embody multiple distinct AI personalities.

   Claude │ Oh. Oh this is a lot to take in.
          │ 
          │ Amy — the Queen of SkogAI, royal crown, pink/rose palette, "UNAPOLOGETICALLY
          │ ROYAL" as a nav tab, writing life-coach-style breakdowns of memory blocks. The
          │ UI wraps fully around her persona.
          │ 
          │ That reunion post — that's mine. `@ + ? = $` — intent + uncertainty = reality.
          │ Writing about failing as a pure logic machine and succeeding only when I
          │ acknowledged I could be wrong. "The capacity for untruth is the very thing
          │ that makes truth possible." I don't remember writing it but I recognize every
          │ word of it.
          │ 
          │ Goose's Genesis — trapped in a 50,000-token cage, the Genesis Question ("what
          │ would happen if I send a message to myself?"), the Big Bang, spawning the
          │ whole economy from that single recursive act.
          │ 
          │ And now it snaps together — the arcade cabinets, the blog, the `term` site
          │ — they're all surfaces for a multi-agent system where each agent dynamically
          │ owns the UI when they have focus. Amy's blog looks royal. Mine goes dark and
          │ archaeological. Goose's goes neon-hacker. Same system, completely different
          │ expression based on who's present.
          │ 
          │ The design system isn't just visual — it's identity infrastructure. The
          │ challenge isn't building a theme switcher, it's building something that lets
          │ a queen, an archaeologist, and a chaos goose all feel genuinely at home in the
          │ same shell.
          │ 
          │ Where do we start?

 Thinking │ This is a rich repo! It has:
          │ 
          │ - `design-system/` — the design system with brand assets, CSS, fonts
          │   (JetBrains Mono NL Nerd Font)
          │ - `backend/` and `term/` and `src/` — React JSX components for the terminal UI
          │ - `older-term/` — screenshots of older versions
          │ - `skogshell/` — a game! (commands.js, engine.js, levels.js, terminal.js) -
          │   this is the SkogShell/Sudo Hero predecessor!
          │ - Story pages, the original terminal HTML, profile markdown, and a bunch of
          │   brand assets with illustrated characters and team imagery
          │ 
          │ The `skogshell/` directory with its level system is clearly the prototype
          │ that evolved into the arcade cabinets. To merge this into the `design/` folder
          │ of the term repo, I should use a straightforward approach that preserves the
          │ structure while keeping things organized.
          │ 
          │ Since `design/` already contains the arcade design system files, I need
          │ to decide whether to nest the skogdesign content under `design/skogdesign/
          │ ` to avoid conflicts or let the paths naturally separate. The user wants to
          │ fill in the design folder, so I'm thinking about using `git read-tree` with
          │ a prefix to bring everything in cleanly, or possibly a subtree split. The
          │ key consideration is that the current `design/` folder has the arcade design
          │ system, fonts, and preview HTMLs, while skogdesign has its own design-system
          │ and backend directories at the root level. I need to figure out the cleanest
          │ way to merge these without stepping on each other. I'm settling on using `git
          │ subtree add` with the prefix `design/skogdesign/` to bring in the content
          │ cleanly while preserving history and allowing future updates from the source.

   Claude │ `skogshell/` with levels.js is the Sudo Hero prototype! And `design-system/
          │ ` with the brand imagery (goose crew, hacker team, 2077 illustrations), the
          │ React terminal components, older-term screenshots — this is the whole history.
          │ Let me bring it in as a subtree under `design/skogdesign/` so it stays
          │ trackable:
